does: MASCing presents the first framework for reconfiguring Mixture-of-Experts behavior in large language models without retraining by using activation steering masks. The method trains an LSTM-based surrogate model to learn how routing decisions across layers influence downstream behaviors and safety outcomes. It then optimizes a steering matrix that identifies behavior-relevant expert circuits, which is used at inference time to apply masks to routing gates and redirect expert selection. This enables rapid adaptation of the same MoE model across different safety scenarios by modulating which experts activate for each input, achieving flexible behavior control while maintaining the efficiency benefits of sparse activation.
connects: This work builds on recent research highlighting safety challenges in sparse expert systems where routing decisions create opaque behavioral couplings. It extends prior work on mechanistic interpretability of neural networks by focusing specifically on expert routing as a control surface. The approach combines insights from parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods and activation editing techniques, adapting surrogacy-based approaches from mechanistic interpretability to the MoE routing domain. It responds to the broader problem of rapidly configuring foundation models for different safety objectives without expensive full retraining, connecting to literature on model safety, interpretability of sparse systems, and efficient adaptation of large models.
enables: MASCing opens pathways for dynamic, scenario-dependent model reconfiguration during deployment without computational overhead of retraining. It enables developers to rapidly switch between safety configurations for different use cases or user populations by adjusting steering masks at inference time. The framework could facilitate research into understanding expert specialization and routing mechanisms in large sparse models. Future work could extend this to other sparse architectures, combine steering with other safety methods like RLHF, or apply the surrogate-modeling approach to other latent control mechanisms in foundation models, potentially establishing activation steering as a general technique for post-hoc model adaptation.
